steps.title: Identify the pairing rule for em-dashes around appositives
steps.content: The opening em-dash before “the point in the sky from which...” introduces an appositive phrase describing the radiant. When an appositive phrase opens with an em-dash, it must close with a matching em-dash. The appositive phrase ends after “emanate,” so the closing em-dash belongs immediately after this word.

steps.title: Apply the pairing rule to the answer choices
steps.content: Choice B correctly closes the appositive with an em-dash: “emanate—is 39 degrees,” allowing the main clause to continue with its predicate. The comma after “is 39 degrees” properly precedes the elaborating phrase “meaning that...” Choices A and D violate the pairing rule by using commas instead of an em-dash after “emanate,” and choice C omits closing punctuation entirely, leaving the paired dashes broken. That matches choice B — B is correct.
